excerpt: These nine surahs are indicative of the Quorum (Mutawada), wherein the forms established in 9,000 years (3,000 years before and 6,000 years within the cycle) were replaced by newly established forms.
source_location_hint: Establishment of cosmic cycles and diacritical correspondence

excerpt: The Prophet was aided in the Battle of Badr by these 9,000 trumpets. When Imam al-Husayn heard the sound of the trumpet on the day of Ashura, it was the same 8,000-year-old sound.
source_location_hint: Correspondence between prophetic miracles and Imam stations

excerpt: The command of Allāh (Amr Allāh) manifests through the Commander of the Faithful (Amīr al-Muʾminīn). The appearance of the Amīr in this period is partial; do not hasten for their full manifestation.
source_work: al-Masabih fi Ithbat al-Imama; Tanbih al-Hadi
source_location_hint: Doctrine of the Qāʾim and partial manifestation

excerpt: When obligatory and supererogatory night prayers are combined, they total twenty-nine rakʿāt, corresponding to the twenty-ninth Imam and the cosmic structure of revelation.
source_authority: high confidence - Ismaili juridical and esoteric tradition
source_location_hint: Numerical correspondences in religious practice and Imamate

excerpt: The twenty diacriticals refer to twenty Imams from al-Hasan to al-Mansur. With the twenty-first Imam al-Ṭayyib, the era of Tayyibi Imamate and the period of occultation began.
source_location_hint: Enumeration of Imams and transition to occultation

excerpt: The Blessed Prophet established twenty-two as the total of daily obligatory prayers, signifying that after twenty Imams, the Imamate would enter concealment, and at its conclusion, the Imam would appear.
source_work: al-Masabih fi Ithbat al-Imama; Kitab al-Yanabiʿ
source_authority: high confidence - Ismaili numerology and prophecy

excerpt: The verses containing Rāʾ appear five times, signifying the Five Purified Lights (Anwār Khamsā) and their correspondence to the five prophets (Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus) and the Panjatān.
source_authority: high confidence - Ismaili doctrine of prophetic succession

excerpt: The verse beginning with Khāʾ contains five letters and appears once, indicating that the Prophet gathered the Five Letters of the Qur'an and the Panjatān al-ʿAthar (Five Purified Ones of Trace).
source_work: al-Masabih fi Ithbat al-Imama; Tanbih al-Hadi

excerpt: Imam al-Ḥasan and Imam al-Ḥusayn stood simultaneously in the prophetic tradition. In the sanctuary of al-Ḥusayn, al-Ḥasan dwells in the station of the heart.
source_location_hint: Doctrine of simultaneous Imams and sanctuary stations

excerpt: Imam al-Ṭayyib gathered not only his own week but also the previous week. He received the Imamate's sanctuary when only three days old. He is the twenty-first Imam and the gatherer of all twenty-one Imams preceding him.
source_authority: high confidence - Tayyibi Ismaili historical narrative
source_location_hint: Life and station of Imam al-Ṭayyib al-ʿAqīl

excerpt: The Qāʾim shall endure ten thousand years, corresponding to the numerical value of Qāf (100). The most auspicious time to recite Surah Qāf is Laylat al-Jumʿa (Thursday evening).
source_location_hint: Correspondence between letters, time, and spiritual practice

excerpt: The diacritical Nūn indicates the gathering of fifty thousand years of lights. Mawlānā ʿAbd al-Muṭṭalib's countenance shone with the radiance of the revelatory period (fifty thousand years).
source_work: al-Masabih fi Ithbat al-Imama; al-Aqwal al-Dhahabiyya
source_authority: high confidence - Ismaili cosmology and spiritual illumination
source_location_hint: Doctrine of ancestral luminosity and Imamate

excerpt: Surah Yāsīn appears in the twenty-third unit of the Qur'an. Laylat al-Qadr manifests in the twenty-third night of Ramadan. Imam al-Ṭayyib occupies this station of the heart.
source_location_hint: Correspondence between Qur'anic divisions and Imam stations

excerpt: The Five Purified Lights first converged in Muḥammad Muṣṭafā. Upon his death, they passed entirely to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib. Subsequently distributed to al-Ḥasan and al-Ḥusayn, they converged again in ʿAlī Zayn al-ʿĀbidīn, then in ʿAlī al-Ṭayyib, and finally shall be gathered by the Qāʾim.
source_work: al-Riyad; Taʾwil al-Daʿaʾim; al-Masabih fi Ithbat al-Imama
source_authority: high confidence - Central Ismaili doctrine of anwār
source_location_hint: Theory of luminous emanation and Imam succession

excerpt: The names of the Speakers (Prophets) are composed of luminous letters (ḥurūf nūrāniyya) and obscure letters (ḥurūf ẓulmāniyya). The Qāʾim possesses only letters of light, constituting the soul opposed to the Speaker's office.
source_authority: high confidence - Ismaili mystical linguistics and cosmology
source_location_hint: Mystical significance of names and letters

excerpt: When Jews mocked the number of angels (nineteen), a wrestler declared he would lift the Prophet on his shoulders on the seventeenth. This verse was revealed in response.
source_location_hint: Story of nineteen angels and its mystical significance

excerpt: The verse 'We shall show them Our signs on the horizons and within their own souls until they recognize that it is the truth from their Lord' contains nineteen letters in its entirety.

From the fourteen diacritical letters, only one coherent statement may be formed: 'The Path of ʿAlī is Truth; adhere to it.' When fourteen letters, fourteen methods, and twenty-nine surahs are summed, the total is fifty-seven, which equals 19 × 3.
source_authority: high confidence - Ismaili sacred mathematics and doctrine of ʿAlī
source_location_hint: Numerical proof of the Wilāya of ʿAlī
